FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: "National Institute of Mental Health (NIMH) Data Archive Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) collection"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with exploratory factor analysis and linear mixed effect models examining behavioral and imaging measures at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in Journal of Adolescent Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time analyzed as generic composite factor (Factor 5: "Screen time exposure" weekday/weekend) with no phone/SM-specific breakout; composite treatment without isolation of modality; fails Step 2 (includes unspecified screen modalities without separate extraction)

notes: Paper focuses on childhood adversities (nutrition, prenatal exposure, ACEs) and screen time as risk factors for maladaptive behaviors using fMRI. Screen time is not isolated as phone/SM-specific; treated as a generic multi-device exposure variable in composite risk model.

FT_IC1.evidence: "We used data from 3498 U.S. adolescents aged 9–11 years with overweight/obesity (45.1% female), from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective longitudinal analysis using Cox proportional hazards models examining associations of sleep duration and screen time with transition from overweight/obesity to normal BMI percentile

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in International Journal of Obesity

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time composite includes "viewing/streaming TV shows or movies, watching/streaming videos (e.g., YouTube), playing video games, texting, video chatting (e.g., Skype, FaceTime), and using social media" without separate per-device breakout; fails Step 2 (multi-device aggregation including TV and video games without isolated modality-specific estimate)

notes: Prospective design with longitudinal follow-up (baseline to Year 2, median 657 days). Screen time measured as composite across multiple modalities; main results report composite screen time associations with BMI transition. No isolated phone/SM-specific variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: "Data are from 7445 participants (47.3% females) aged 9-10 years at baseline (2016-2018) in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective longitudinal analysis using weighted multivariable linear regression models examining independent and joint associations of screen time and sleep disturbance with BMI and waist circumference at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article in Childhood Obesity

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time composite from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey includes "TV shows/movies, video games, and socializing (texting, video chatting, and social media)" with main analysis reporting composite screen time categories (0–4, >4–8, >8 hours/day) without isolated phone/SM-specific coefficients in primary results; fails Step 2 (multi-device aggregation including TV and video games without separate breakout in main or visible supplementary results)

notes: Prospective longitudinal design (2-year follow-up from 2016-2018 baseline). Primary analysis uses composite screen time (continuous and categorical 4-hour intervals). Paper mentions analysis "by type" but main Table 2 reports only composite screen time associations. No isolated phone/SM-specific variable in primary results.
